title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Runtime kernel selection caused latency spike (2026-03-18)
preview: A new kernel selection heuristic preferred a kernel variant that performed poorly for a specific sequence length distribution on A100s, causing latency regressions.
text: A new kernel selection heuristic preferred a kernel variant that performed poorly for a specific sequence length distribution on A100s, causing latency regressions.

title: How-to: Integrate Optimize with the Cost Model API (What-if projections + ROI)
preview:
## Purpose
This guide explains how Redwood Optimize should call the Cost Model API v1 “what-if” endpoints to produce consistent, defensible ROI and savings estimates across batching, caching, and quantization recommendations.

It covers:
- How to construct workload summaries (aggregated inputs) instead of per-reques...
text:
## Purpose
This guide explains how Redwood Optimize should call the Cost Model API v1 “what-if” endpoints to produce consistent, defensible ROI and savings estimates across batching, caching, and quantization recommendations.

It covers:
- How to construct workload summaries (aggregated inputs) instead of per-request payloads
- How to call the what-if projection endpoint(s) safely (bounds, timeouts, retries)
- How to interpret confidence intervals and communicate uncertainty
- Guardrails: when *not* to show ROI, and how to avoid over-optimistic recommendations

Optimize should treat the Cost Model API as the canonical source for cost primitives and projections. Avoid re-implementing cost math in Optimize except for UI presentation and product logic.

---

## Audience
- Optimize backend + recommendation pipeline engineers
- Applied ML (quantization / caching / batching ROI modeling)
- ...

title: Runbook: Audit Log Coverage Regression (Missing Sensitive-Action Events)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to respond when Redwood’s audit logging coverage regresses (e.g., a newly added/changed admin endpoint or workflow no longer emits a required audit event, or emits an event that fails schema/taxonomy requirements).

Coverage regressions are treated as security-impacting quality ...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to respond when Redwood’s audit logging coverage regresses (e.g., a newly added/changed admin endpoint or workflow no longer emits a required audit event, or emits an event that fails schema/taxonomy requirements).

Coverage regressions are treated as security-impacting quality issues because they can break customer detection/investigation workflows and SOC2/ISO-aligned evidence expectations.

## When to use this runbook
Use this runbook when any of the following occurs:
- The Audit Coverage dashboard shows a drop in observed coverage for one or more `event_type`s.
- An alert fires for missing expected event types for a sensitive action.
- Support/Solutions reports a customer issue: “action happened, but we don’t see a corresponding audit event” (common in Private and Console workflows).
- CI begins failing due to new/changed code paths missing re...

title: Cost Model Service (cost-model-api) — Service Overview
preview:
## Summary
Cost Model Service (service name: **cost-model-api**) provides a single, versioned implementation for inference cost estimation and cost breakdowns across Redwood products.

It is the canonical source for:
- **Per-request cost estimates** and standardized breakdown taxonomy (prefill/decode, cache, batch e...
text:
## Summary
Cost Model Service (service name: **cost-model-api**) provides a single, versioned implementation for inference cost estimation and cost breakdowns across Redwood products.

It is the canonical source for:
- **Per-request cost estimates** and standardized breakdown taxonomy (prefill/decode, cache, batch efficiency, overhead allocation, quantization deltas)
- **Aggregated cost breakdowns** for Console dashboards (bounded-cardinality responses)
- **What-if projections** used by Optimize to quantify ROI from configuration changes (batching/caching/quantization)

This document describes architecture, dependencies, SLOs, ownership/on-call, and rollout strategy.

---

## Service owners
**Primary owner team:** Platform Control Plane
- Service/API lead: Ethan Park
- Control plane integration: Benji Okafor, Cole Summers
- Auth/tenancy: Kaitlyn Nguyen

**Reliability partner:** SRE
- ...

title: ADR-021: Standardize Cost Model Interfaces (Canonical Spec + Cost Model API)
preview:
## Status
Accepted (Published)

## Context
Redwood Inference currently has multiple, partially overlapping ways to represent and compute inference cost:
- Console prototypes compute cost breakdowns from telemetry aggregation jobs.
- Optimize prototypes compute “what-if” ROI using separate assumptions and field names...
text:
## Status
Accepted (Published)

## Context
Redwood Inference currently has multiple, partially overlapping ways to represent and compute inference cost:
- Console prototypes compute cost breakdowns from telemetry aggregation jobs.
- Optimize prototypes compute “what-if” ROI using separate assumptions and field names.
- Billing/quota hooks use yet another set of primitives and accounting splits.

This has led to:
- Inconsistent customer-facing numbers (Console vs invoice) and inconsistent internal reporting.
- Unclear semantics for “what counts” (prefill vs decode, cache hits, batch efficiency, quantization impact, and overhead).
- Duplicated engineering effort and fragile integrations when any one surface evolves.
- Operational risk: drift investigations are slowed by lack of a single “source of truth” representation.

We need to unify inputs/outputs so Console (dashboards/observabili...

title: Cost Model API v1 — Design Spec
preview:
## Summary
This document specifies Cost Model API v1 for Redwood Inference. The API provides a single, canonical interface to compute and serve standardized inference cost primitives for:
- Redwood Console (dashboards, cost breakdown views)
- Redwood Optimize (what-if projections and recommendation ROI estimates)
- ...
text:
## Summary
This document specifies Cost Model API v1 for Redwood Inference. The API provides a single, canonical interface to compute and serve standardized inference cost primitives for:
- Redwood Console (dashboards, cost breakdown views)
- Redwood Optimize (what-if projections and recommendation ROI estimates)
- Finance/unit-economics reporting (through shared primitives; not the billing system of record)

The v1 API is internal-only (service-to-service) and is designed to be:
- Deterministic (given identical inputs, schema version, and policy)
- Bounded (responses and computation time must remain bounded under high-cardinality workloads)
- Versioned (schema + semantics are versioned; compatible evolution is enforced)

## Goals (v1)
1) Provide stable endpoints for:
 - Per-request cost estimate
 - Aggregated cost breakdowns over a time window
 - What-if projections for config change...

title: ADR-022: Standard Cost Breakdown Taxonomy (v1)
preview:
## Status
Published (v1). Any changes require an ADR addendum or ADR-022 revision.

## Context
Redwood Console and Redwood Optimize both present “cost” to users, while telemetry pipelines and billing hooks compute similar-but-not-identical numbers. This has caused:
- Conflicting prefill vs decode splits (runtime vs ...
text:
## Status
Published (v1). Any changes require an ADR addendum or ADR-022 revision.

## Context
Redwood Console and Redwood Optimize both present “cost” to users, while telemetry pipelines and billing hooks compute similar-but-not-identical numbers. This has caused:
- Conflicting prefill vs decode splits (runtime vs analytics derived).
- Ambiguous treatment of KV/prefix cache hits (are they “free” or “discounted”?).
- Batch efficiency treated as either a per-request discount or as fleet-level overhead.
- Quantization changes reported without consistent deltas and guardrails.
- Overhead allocation handled differently between finance reporting, Console views, and Optimize ROI.

This ADR defines a canonical taxonomy and semantics for cost breakdown outputs used by the Standard Cost Model spec and Cost Model API.

## Decision
We will standardize on a **two-level cost breakdown taxonomy** w...

title: Optimize Quality Gates — System Design (v1)
preview:
## Summary
This document describes the end-to-end system design for **Quality Guardrails Gating for Optimize**. The feature ensures that any Optimize recommendation that changes serving behavior (batching policy, prefix/KV cache settings, quantization profiles) is **blocked from production traffic** until it passes ...
text:
## Summary
This document describes the end-to-end system design for **Quality Guardrails Gating for Optimize**. The feature ensures that any Optimize recommendation that changes serving behavior (batching policy, prefix/KV cache settings, quantization profiles) is **blocked from production traffic** until it passes required quality evaluations (offline gates + optional online canary signals). The design includes:
- Service boundaries and interactions (Optimize, eval-harness, Console, Rollout Controller)
- Data model for gate runs and baselines
- APIs and contracts
- Gate run state machine: `pending` → `running` → `passed|failed` with `canceled|timed_out` and `overridden`
- Failure modes, retries, and operational guardrails

## Goals
1. **Prevent silent quality regressions** from cost optimizations.
2. Provide **consistent pass/fail gates** with statistical handling for variance and fl...

title: Region Health Service (RHS) — Aggregated Region Health for Routing and Failover
preview:
## Overview
The Region Health Service (RHS) provides a single, consistent, low-latency view of per-region health for Redwood’s multi-region routing and failover automation. RHS aggregates multiple signals (reachability, error/latency burn, capacity/headroom, and control-plane dependencies) into a normalized region s...
text:
## Overview
The Region Health Service (RHS) provides a single, consistent, low-latency view of per-region health for Redwood’s multi-region routing and failover automation. RHS aggregates multiple signals (reachability, error/latency burn, capacity/headroom, and control-plane dependencies) into a normalized region status with explainability ("why we think a region is unhealthy").

RHS is designed to:
- Reduce failover trigger noise by requiring corroboration and applying dampening/hold-down logic upstream of policy changes.
- Provide deterministic inputs for the routing policy engine and the failover controller.
- Provide an auditable “evidence bundle” to support on-call investigation and post-incident reviews.

**Primary consumers**
- Smart Routing / Policy Engine (real-time routing decisions)
- Failover Controller (automation decisioning + execution)
- Observability dashboards/alert...

text:
## Overview
This page defines the supported **upgrade** and **rollback** paths for **Redwood Private offline bundles** and how these paths interact with:
- **Signature verification** (fail-closed by default)
- **Trust store / key rotation** (multiple trusted public keys)
- **Downgrade prevention** (signed version metadata + local install state)

This matrix is used by Solutions Engineering, Support, and SRE to qualify upgrade requests in air-gapped environments and to standardize rollback guidance.

### Definitions (terms used on this page)
- **Offline bundle**: A tar/zip bundle that contains charts/manifests, container image references and/or image tarballs, runtime/model artifacts, and a **canonical manifest** with hashes.
- **Signed manifest**: The canonical manifest plus a signature envelope (detached `manifest.sig` or equivalent) produced by Redwood Release Engineering.
- **Insta...

title: Hardware tuning profiles: schema + compatibility rules
preview:
## Summary
This document defines the **hardware tuning profile schema** and the **compatibility/guardrail rules** used by the serving runtime to apply safe, versioned tuning presets across GPU SKUs (H100, B200, L40S) and across deployment modes (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).

The schema is designed to:
- Keep profile...
text:
## Summary
This document defines the **hardware tuning profile schema** and the **compatibility/guardrail rules** used by the serving runtime to apply safe, versioned tuning presets across GPU SKUs (H100, B200, L40S) and across deployment modes (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).

The schema is designed to:
- Keep profile changes **safe-by-default** (strict validation, bounded knobs, explicit intent).
- Make profile application **deterministic and debuggable** (explicit selection logic, metrics, logs).
- Prevent **silent drift** between runtime defaults and shipped profile packs (normalized defaults + compatibility constraints).

This is a design reference for implementation in `runtime-config` and the profile loader.

---

## Goals
1. Define a **profile pack format** with versioning and a stable schema.
2. Support **inheritance/overrides** to minimize duplication (common base → GPU family ...

title: Incident Handover, Shadowing, and Escalation Cookbook
preview:

This cookbook documents the standard process for on-call handovers, structured shadowing, and incident escalation at Redwood Inference. It consolidates the operational steps, checklists, templates, and example runbook fragments teams should adopt to reduce knowledge gaps during rotation changes, accelerate...
text:
Summary:

This cookbook documents the standard process for on-call handovers, structured shadowing, and incident escalation at Redwood Inference. It consolidates the operational steps, checklists, templates, and example runbook fragments teams should adopt to reduce knowledge gaps during rotation changes, accelerate TTR, and ensure consistent customer communications.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to all engineering teams participating in the platform on-call rotation (Serving Runtime, SRE, Platform, and Private Deployments support sub-teams).
- Covers: shift handovers, 24–72 hour shadowing plans for new responders, live incident escalation paths, internal and external communication templates, and post-incident handoff into remediation work.

Goals:
- Minimize context loss between shifts and during escalations.
- Ensure first-response confidence through structured shadowing and d...

title: Optimization Config Service (OCS)
preview:
## Overview
Optimization Config Service (OCS) is the control-plane service responsible for **versioning, validating, and safely rolling out** optimization configuration changes across Redwood’s inference fleet.

Optimization configs cover settings that affect runtime economics and performance, including:
- **Batchin...
text:
## Overview
Optimization Config Service (OCS) is the control-plane service responsible for **versioning, validating, and safely rolling out** optimization configuration changes across Redwood’s inference fleet.

Optimization configs cover settings that affect runtime economics and performance, including:
- **Batching** (heuristics, max batch tokens/size, queue timeouts)
- **Caching** (prefix/KV cache enablement, cache sizing policies)
- **Quantization profiles** (profile selection, compatibility constraints, quality gating)

OCS implements:
- Immutable **Optimization Config Version** objects with metadata and diffs
- Safe apply workflows (validate → canary → promote)
- One-click rollback (Console + API)
- Audit logging and RBAC enforcement

OCS supports **Hosted** and **Dedicated** fully managed via Redwood control plane, and provides the same versioning model for **Private** deployme...

title: Offline bundle signing + verification design (Redwood Private on-prem)
preview:
## Overview
This document defines the engineering design for cryptographically signing Redwood Private offline bundles and verifying them during air-gapped installation and upgrade.

We are solving two problems:
1) **Integrity + provenance** for offline artifacts (bundle content cannot be modified without detection)...
text:
## Overview
This document defines the engineering design for cryptographically signing Redwood Private offline bundles and verifying them during air-gapped installation and upgrade.

We are solving two problems:
1) **Integrity + provenance** for offline artifacts (bundle content cannot be modified without detection).
2) **Safe operational UX** in air-gapped environments (clear failures, predictable rotation, break-glass path).

This design intentionally piggybacks on the existing on-prem/offline bundle installer architecture. It does not redesign packaging, registry mirroring, or Helm chart structure.

---

## Goals
- Sign offline bundles in the Redwood release pipeline using protected keys and auditable steps.
- Verify signatures and artifact hashes during installer preflight, failing closed by default.
- Support key rotation without bricking offline customers (multi-key trust store)...

title: Emergency release checklist (runtime + platform)
preview:
# Purpose
This checklist is used when a release must be cut quickly to mitigate an incident. It defines what can be expedited and what must not be skipped.

# What you can expedite
- You may reduce test scope to *targeted* suites for the impacted component.
- You may use an existing approved base image if rebuilding...
text:
# Purpose
This checklist is used when a release must be cut quickly to mitigate an incident. It defines what can be expedited and what must not be skipped.

# What you can expedite
- You may reduce test scope to *targeted* suites for the impacted component.
- You may use an existing approved base image if rebuilding would delay mitigation.
- You may merge directly to a release branch if main is currently unstable (with approvals).

# What you must not skip
1. Artifact provenance: build must be from a known branch and pinned commit SHA.
2. Unit tests relevant to the touched area must be run (or justified and approved).
3. Security scanning must run (SAST + dependency scan) unless Security Oncall explicitly approves bypass.
4. Rollback plan must be written before deploy.

# Runtime emergency release steps
1. **Branching**
 - Create `hotfix/<short-desc>` from `release/runtime/<yyyy-mm-dd...

title: ADR-015: Admission control layering for hosted overload protection (Gateway vs Routing vs Runtime)
preview:
## Status
Published (effective for Hosted API overload protection rollout).

## Context
Redwood Inference’s Hosted API sees bursty and highly variable traffic (prompt length variance, streaming concurrency spikes, regional capacity dips). During overload, failures can cascade into:
- Tail-latency blowups (p95/p99), ...
text:
## Status
Published (effective for Hosted API overload protection rollout).

## Context
Redwood Inference’s Hosted API sees bursty and highly variable traffic (prompt length variance, streaming concurrency spikes, regional capacity dips). During overload, failures can cascade into:
- Tail-latency blowups (p95/p99), then timeouts
- Elevated 5xx from upstream retry storms and downstream saturation
- Noisy-neighbor effects across tenants and tiers
- Confusing customer experience (timeouts and generic 5xx instead of stable semantics)

We need a layered admission control strategy that:
1) Rejects or degrades traffic early and predictably.
2) Preserves tenant isolation and tier-aware behavior.
3) Avoids feedback loops (e.g., shedding causing routing thrash, or routing failover amplifying load elsewhere).
4) Produces consistent customer-facing overload errors (see ADR-014).

This ADR defines...

title: ADR-014: Hosted overload error taxonomy (customer-facing)
preview:
## Summary
This ADR defines the **customer-facing error taxonomy** for overload protection in the Redwood Hosted API, including:
- Stable **error codes** and their semantics
- HTTP **status mapping** (primarily 429 vs 503)
- Standard JSON **response payload shape** (additive and backwards compatible)
- **Retry-After...
text:
## Summary
This ADR defines the **customer-facing error taxonomy** for overload protection in the Redwood Hosted API, including:
- Stable **error codes** and their semantics
- HTTP **status mapping** (primarily 429 vs 503)
- Standard JSON **response payload shape** (additive and backwards compatible)
- **Retry-After** header semantics and client retry guidance

This taxonomy is used by the API Gateway admission layer, Smart Routing, and serving runtime admission points.

## Context / Problem
The Hosted API experiences bursty workloads. During saturation, failures have historically presented as ambiguous timeouts and generic 5xx errors, causing:
- Retry storms and further overload
- Confusing customer experience (unclear whether to retry, when, and how)
- Difficult incident triage due to inconsistent error signatures across services

We need **stable, explicit overload errors** so:
- T...

title: ADR-007: Standard KMS Client Wrapper and Failure Policy
preview:
## Status
Published.

## Summary
We will standardize all Key Management Service (KMS/HSM-backed) calls in the Redwood monorepo behind a shared **KMS client wrapper** with:
- Explicit, consistent **timeouts** and **retry budgets** (with jitter/backoff).
- A canonical **error taxonomy** used across logs, metrics, trac...
text:
## Status
Published.

## Summary
We will standardize all Key Management Service (KMS/HSM-backed) calls in the Redwood monorepo behind a shared **KMS client wrapper** with:
- Explicit, consistent **timeouts** and **retry budgets** (with jitter/backoff).
- A canonical **error taxonomy** used across logs, metrics, traces, and user-facing/operator-facing messages.
- A shared **circuit breaker** and **backpressure** policy to prevent retry storms and cascading failures when KMS is degraded.
- Strict **caching constraints** and safe defaults appropriate for envelope encryption.

This ADR defines the wrapper API shape, failure-handling rules, observability requirements, and non-goals.

## Context / Problem
Redwood’s Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments all integrate with customer-managed KMS/HSM for envelope encryption flows (e.g., DEK generation/encryption, DEK decryption, key metadat...

Applies to any cus...
text:
## Purpose
This checklist is the required go/no-go gate for upgrading an *enterprise* Redwood Private deployment (VPC or on-prem). It defines the minimum validations, evidence, and sign-offs required before starting the maintenance window, and the stop/rollback criteria during execution.

## Scope
Applies to any customer production Private environment upgrade of: (a) control plane components (operator, controller, API gateway), (b) serving runtime/data plane images, (c) cluster dependencies shipped by Redwood (ingress, cert-manager bundles, logging agents), or (d) installer/upgrade tooling.

> Policy dependencies: This page is the operational gate, but approvals/comms requirements are defined in the Change Management Policy and Planned Maintenance Comms SOP. Security-specific sign-off requirements are defined in Secure SDLC: Security Sign-off for Customer Deployments. SLO/error budget...

title: ADR: Optimization Config Versioning Model (Immutable Versions + Scoped Pointers)
preview:
## Status
Published (approved in Architecture Review on 2025-10-21).

## Context
Redwood Optimize supports a set of operational inference optimizations (batching heuristics, caching toggles/parameters, and quantization profiles). Historically, optimization changes have been applied as mutable configuration updates. ...
text:
## Status
Published (approved in Architecture Review on 2025-10-21).

## Context
Redwood Optimize supports a set of operational inference optimizations (batching heuristics, caching toggles/parameters, and quantization profiles). Historically, optimization changes have been applied as mutable configuration updates. This has caused:
- Limited traceability (difficult to answer “what changed, by whom, and where is it active?”).
- Higher change-failure risk (hard to stage safely across tenant/model/region).
- Incomplete rollback semantics (partial worker apply, race conditions, and unclear “current” config).

This ADR defines the **versioning model** for Optimization Configs in the control plane, including:
- Immutable version objects vs mutable config objects.
- How “active config” is represented (pointers/bindings).
- Promotion semantics and environment scoping.

The model must support ...

title: Capacity Policy Service (CPS) — Control Plane service for scaling policy evaluation
preview:
## Overview
Capacity Policy Service (CPS) is an internal control plane service responsible for storing, validating, and *deterministically evaluating* per-deployment scaling policies for **Redwood Dedicated**.

CPS exists to ensure consistent, auditable scaling semantics across:
- Console + API configuration (writes...
text:
## Overview
Capacity Policy Service (CPS) is an internal control plane service responsible for storing, validating, and *deterministically evaluating* per-deployment scaling policies for **Redwood Dedicated**.

CPS exists to ensure consistent, auditable scaling semantics across:
- Console + API configuration (writes)
- Fleet autoscaler loops (reads/evaluations)
- Smart routing and downstream consumers that need a canonical “bounded” capacity state

**Primary responsibilities**
1. Persist per-deployment scaling policy configuration (min/max bounds, cooldowns, predictive mode settings).
2. Provide an evaluation API that resolves **effective policy** (customer config + platform defaults + feature flags) and returns a canonical, deterministic result.
3. Emit audit events for config changes and evaluations (who/when/what/why) to support enterprise change management.
4. Provide caching and ...

title: Postmortem: Private VPC egress disruption and region fallback (2025-07-22)
preview:
h2. Summary
On 2025-07-22, a networking change in a customer-controlled VPC (Private deployment) disrupted outbound connectivity to one of the inference subnets. As a mitigation, the customer and Redwood jointly activated *automatic region fallback* to a secondary region for non-residency-bound traffic until routing...
text:
h2. Summary
On 2025-07-22, a networking change in a customer-controlled VPC (Private deployment) disrupted outbound connectivity to one of the inference subnets. As a mitigation, the customer and Redwood jointly activated *automatic region fallback* to a secondary region for non-residency-bound traffic until routing was corrected.

h2. Impact
* Customer: 1 Private deployment (customer-sensitive).
* Symptoms: intermittent timeouts and 502/504 on a subset of inference nodes.
* Duration: ~2 hours of partial impairment; user-visible errors concentrated in the first 35 minutes.

h2. Timeline (UTC)
* 16:20: Customer reports timeouts on chat endpoints.
* 16:27: Redwood on-call engaged; traces show failed egress from subnet private-inf-az2.
* 16:41: Mitigation: enabled *region fallback* for allowed routes to shift traffic from us-east-1 (primary) to us-west-2 (secondary).
* 17:05: Error rate ...

title: ADR: Private upgrade plan architecture + rollback contract
preview:
## Status
Published (effective starting Private release train 2025.06).

## Decision summary
We will implement Private upgrades as a **two-step, plan-driven workflow**:
1) **Generate** a deterministic, versioned **Upgrade Plan** (declarative plan artifact) after running preflight validation and artifact availability...
text:
## Status
Published (effective starting Private release train 2025.06).

## Decision summary
We will implement Private upgrades as a **two-step, plan-driven workflow**:
1) **Generate** a deterministic, versioned **Upgrade Plan** (declarative plan artifact) after running preflight validation and artifact availability checks.
2) **Execute** the plan using the installer state machine, persisting step outcomes and emitting audit events.

Rollback will be supported through a **defined rollback contract**:
- Rollback is executed by consuming the persisted upgrade state + plan.
- Rollback is **best-effort and deterministic** within the supported contract (see “Rollback contract”).
- We will provide explicit, operator-friendly “manual intervention required” guidance for scenarios outside the contract.

We explicitly reject an imperative-only “do upgrade now” approach and an “always blue/green...

title: Burst Admission Controller (BAC) Service
preview:
## Summary
The Burst Admission Controller (BAC) is an internal, regional control-plane service that makes deterministic, low-latency admission decisions for Dedicated customers attempting to exceed their reserved baseline capacity (“bursting”) by borrowing from Redwood-managed burst pools.

BAC’s core responsibiliti...
text:
## Summary
The Burst Admission Controller (BAC) is an internal, regional control-plane service that makes deterministic, low-latency admission decisions for Dedicated customers attempting to exceed their reserved baseline capacity (“bursting”) by borrowing from Redwood-managed burst pools.

BAC’s core responsibilities are:
- Enforce guardrails (tenant caps, max burst ratios, cooldown windows, per-pool saturation limits, global fleet safety caps).
- Apply fairness across tenants sharing a burst pool (weighted token-bucket / WFQ hybrid; see ADR for selection).
- Produce explainable denial reasons for customers and Support.
- Emit metering signals for billing and operational telemetry for SRE.

Non-goals:
- Scheduling/queuing long-running work (BAC returns immediate decisions; callers may implement client-side or gateway queuing/backoff).
- Cross-region bursting (out of scope for initial...

title: Redwood Private: Upgrade + Rollback Workflow (Overview)
preview:
## Purpose
This page describes the end-to-end, operator-facing upgrade workflow for **Redwood Private** deployments (VPC and on-prem/air-gapped). It is intended as a stable overview of:
- Upgrade phases and sequencing between **control plane** and **data plane**
- Safety invariants (what must be true before we proce...
text:
## Purpose
This page describes the end-to-end, operator-facing upgrade workflow for **Redwood Private** deployments (VPC and on-prem/air-gapped). It is intended as a stable overview of:
- Upgrade phases and sequencing between **control plane** and **data plane**
- Safety invariants (what must be true before we proceed)
- Required inputs and artifacts
- Operator UX (CLI-first, with optional Console visibility)
- Rollback entry points and guarantees

This is the canonical workflow referenced by the installer, Helm packaging, Terraform variables, and support/runbooks.

---

## Definitions
**Private Control Plane (CP)**
- Private management services (API, auth integration, policy, upgrade coordinator) running in the customer cluster/VPC or on-prem environment.

**Private Data Plane (DP)**
- Inference runtime components and worker pools that serve traffic.

**Installer**
- `redwood-private...

title: Private Upgrades: Preflight (Precheck) Specification
preview:
## Purpose
This page specifies **preflight checks ("prechecks")** executed by `redwood-private-installer` before any Redwood Private upgrade steps are performed. The goal is to prevent common upgrade failures by validating environment readiness up front and producing **actionable remediation guidance**.

This spec c...
text:
## Purpose
This page specifies **preflight checks ("prechecks")** executed by `redwood-private-installer` before any Redwood Private upgrade steps are performed. The goal is to prevent common upgrade failures by validating environment readiness up front and producing **actionable remediation guidance**.

This spec covers:
- Check categories and required inputs
- Pass/fail criteria and thresholds
- **Blocking vs warning** behavior
- Standardized error codes, messages, and operator remediation
- Special considerations for **VPC** vs **on-prem/air-gapped** deployments

Non-goals:
- Replacing runtime monitoring during upgrades (see SRE alert runbooks)
- Full coverage for every historical migration edge case (installer will explicitly identify "manual intervention required")

---

## Definitions
- **Private deployment**: Redwood Private control plane + data plane running in customer-contro...

title: Dedicated Burst Capacity: Burst Pool Architecture & Guardrails (Design Spec)
preview:
## 1. Overview
This design specifies how Redwood Dedicated customers can temporarily exceed their reserved baseline GPU allocation by borrowing from Redwood-managed shared “burst pools,” while preserving platform-wide SLOs and protecting non-bursting tenants.

This document covers:
- Burst pool topology (per region ...
text:
## 1. Overview
This design specifies how Redwood Dedicated customers can temporarily exceed their reserved baseline GPU allocation by borrowing from Redwood-managed shared “burst pools,” while preserving platform-wide SLOs and protecting non-bursting tenants.

This document covers:
- Burst pool topology (per region and hardware class)
- Admission control and deterministic decisioning
- Fairness model for shared pools (token bucket / WFQ hybrid)
- Guardrails: per-tenant caps, cooldowns, global fleet safety, circuit breakers
- Routing/data plane integration and fallback behavior
- Failure modes and rollback strategy

Out of scope (initial release):
- Cross-region bursting (burst is constrained within the region)
- Customer-specific dedicated burst pools (initially shared Redwood-managed pools with strict segmentation)
- Market/spot style reservation trading

## 2. Definitions
- **Baseli...

text:
## Summary
The **Experiment Service** (internal name: `experiment-svc`) is the control-plane component that stores and validates route-level experiment configuration and publishes a fast, deterministic representation to the policy/routing layer.

This document covers:
- What `experiment-svc` owns vs. what the policy engine/router owns
- API surface (Console + internal)
- Storage model and versioning
- Validation rules and safety guardrails
- Interaction with the policy engine (config propagation)
- Caching strategy (server-side and data-plane)
- Failure modes and degraded behaviors

## Background and goals
Route-level A/B testing requires customers to define experiments at the **route** boundary (endpoint + traffic policy), assign requests deterministically to a **variant**, and then attribute telemetry by `{route, experiment_id, variant}`.

Control-plane requirements:
- Single source...

title: Dedicated Pool Rollouts Service (Control Plane)

## Overview
The **Dedicated Pool Rollouts Service** (service name: `dedicated-pool-rollouts`) is the control-plane component that manages **staged rollout plans** for **Dedicated GPU capacity pools**. It provides APIs, persistence, and auditability for creating and executing staged rollouts of:

text:
## Overview
The **Dedicated Pool Rollouts Service** (service name: `dedicated-pool-rollouts`) is the control-plane component that manages **staged rollout plans** for **Dedicated GPU capacity pools**. It provides APIs, persistence, and auditability for creating and executing staged rollouts of:
- Model versions (e.g., `llama-3.1-70b@2025-03-xx`)
- Serving runtime builds (e.g., runtime image / kernel set)
- Routing policies (e.g., fallback policy changes)
- Pool-level configuration changes that materially affect production traffic

This service is responsible for **deterministic, auditable, reversible** rollout control. It does **not** directly route traffic; instead it publishes rollout state to the policy/routing layer which enforces stage partitions at request time.

Primary users:
- Redwood SRE/Operators running Dedicated pool upgrades
- Console workflows (customer admins and Redwo...

title: INC-2025-0303 Postmortem: Dedicated autoscaler lag + quota ceiling caused sustained throttling
preview:
# Summary
On 2025-03-03, a set of Dedicated customers saw elevated 429 throttling and increased queueing latency for ~62 minutes. The dedicated autoscaler attempted to add GPU nodes but hit a cloud quota ceiling, while the fallback routing policy was configured to prefer in-region capacity.

**Incident ID:** INC-202...
text:
# Summary
On 2025-03-03, a set of Dedicated customers saw elevated 429 throttling and increased queueing latency for ~62 minutes. The dedicated autoscaler attempted to add GPU nodes but hit a cloud quota ceiling, while the fallback routing policy was configured to prefer in-region capacity.

**Incident ID:** INC-2025-0303
**Customer impact:** Dedicated tier (subset), primarily eu-west
**Owning engineering team:** Eng SRE

# What happened
A scheduled load test by a customer coincided with a new prompt pattern increasing average output length. The autoscaler hit scale limits and then failed to provision due to quota. The system kept retrying, causing prolonged throttling.

# Timeline (UTC)
- 2025-03-03 09:12: Alert: dedicated-throttle-rate > 2%
- 09:17: Eng SRE on-call engaged
- 09:24: Identified provisioning failures due to quota ceiling
- 09:29: Mitigation: temporarily enabled cross-r...

## Overview
Residency Policy Service (RPS) is the control-plane system of record for **Hosted API data residency policies** (region pinning). RPS stores policy objects, enforces validation rules, provides deterministic policy evaluation for in-path services, and emits **audit events** for create/update/delete and ov...
text:
## Overview
Residency Policy Service (RPS) is the control-plane system of record for **Hosted API data residency policies** (region pinning). RPS stores policy objects, enforces validation rules, provides deterministic policy evaluation for in-path services, and emits **audit events** for create/update/delete and overrides.

RPS is part of the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation initiative: **Hosted data residency enforcement**. It enables:
- Customer-configurable **allowed region set** and **primary region** at org/project scope (and optionally API key).
- Deterministic request-time enforcement by downstream services (API Gateway + Smart Routing).
- Auditable configuration changes and operational observability.

RPS does **not** route traffic. It only provides configuration and a consistent evaluation contract.

## Service ownership
- Primary owner: Platform Control Plane (Et...

title: Automatic fallback policy behavior (Hosted API)
preview:
# Summary
This page documents the behavior of **automatic fallback** in the Redwood serving runtime for the **Hosted API**. This policy is used during normal operations and is often enabled during model rollouts to reduce customer-visible errors during capacity or instability events.

# What fallback does
When fallb...
text:
# Summary
This page documents the behavior of **automatic fallback** in the Redwood serving runtime for the **Hosted API**. This policy is used during normal operations and is often enabled during model rollouts to reduce customer-visible errors during capacity or instability events.

# What fallback does
When fallback is enabled on a route, the router may send requests to a **fallback model** instead of the primary model based on defined triggers. The request remains a standard Hosted API request; the router modifies the upstream target only.

# Fallback triggers
Fallback can activate for these reasons (not exhaustive):
- **Capacity pressure**: primary pool is saturated (queue time above threshold).
- **Elevated error rate**: primary 5xx above threshold over a short window.
- **Cold start or rollout warmup**: primary replicas not ready or still ramping.
- **Regional impairment**: a s...

text:
## Incident summary
**Incident ID:** IR-2025-017
**Start:** 2025-02-11
**End:** 2025-02-14
**Severity:** Sev2

Some Redwood Private tenants experienced **dropped audit log events** in the audit-event-router when the downstream sink became slow or intermittently unavailable. This caused **incomplete audit exports** for specific time windows.

## Impact
### Customer impact (confirmed)
The following customer accounts reported missing audit events and were validated by Redwood Support/Engineering:
- HelioPay
- AcmeBank
- Globex
- Umbrella Bio

### Customer impact (unconfirmed)
- Northwind Health (reported informally; pending concrete timestamps at time of writing)

### What was affected
- Audit events related to admin actions (RBAC role bindings, API key lifecycle)
- Export paths using HTTP push sinks were overrepresented, but the core issue was router backpressure handling

## Detection
...

title: ADR-017: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
preview:
## Status
Published (ADR-017)

## Decision summary
Retention policies are evaluated in the **control plane**. Data plane components (log/telemetry stores, purge jobs, exporters) consume a **resolved “effective policy snapshot”** produced by the control plane and do not independently recompute precedence/validation r...
text:
## Status
Published (ADR-017)

## Decision summary
Retention policies are evaluated in the **control plane**. Data plane components (log/telemetry stores, purge jobs, exporters) consume a **resolved “effective policy snapshot”** produced by the control plane and do not independently recompute precedence/validation rules.

We will:
- Centralize evaluation, precedence rules, and validation in the control plane (Retention Policy Service + evaluator module).
- Cache effective policy snapshots in the control plane (in-memory + Redis) with explicit invalidation on policy changes.
- Make policy changes and evaluation outputs auditable via immutable change history and audit log events.

## Context
The “Retention controls + compliance reporting pack” program requires consistent enforcement of customer-configurable retention policies across multiple data classes and deployment modes (Hosted / D...

text:
h1. Context
BluePeak Insurance is evaluating *Redwood Private* for a regulated workload that must run in a *disconnected network* (their wording: "no outbound internet" / "no egress").

h1. Requirement summary (customer stated)
* Deployment mode: Redwood Private (customer-managed Kubernetes).
* Network: *no outbound internet access from the cluster* (no NAT, no direct egress).
* Inbound connectivity: allowed only from BluePeak corporate network over approved VPN.
* Artifact delivery: software/images must be provided via *offline media* or an internal artifact mirror.
* Updates: quarterly upgrade windows; emergency patches require pre-approved delivery procedure.

h1. Implications for Redwood
# Any components that assume outbound calls (license checks, telemetry, update checks) must be disabled or routed to an internal endpoint.
# Container images must be mirrored into BluePeak registr...

text:
# Summary
This standard defines the minimum performance and reliability bar a model must meet before it can be launched in the Hosted API catalog.

# Scope
Applies to: chat/completions models offered in the Hosted API, including OpenAI-compatible routes.
Does not apply to: one-off customer private builds (see Private Deployments standards).

# Owners
- Standard owner: Serving Runtime
- Enforcement: Release Engineering + SRE

# Benchmark methodology (required)
Run the perf suite using the current `perf-canary` harness with:
- Hardware class: `H100-80GB` (default) and one secondary class (e.g., `L40S`) when we expect meaningful traffic
- Context sizes: 2k, 8k, 16k
- Workload: mix of prompt lengths matching the top-10 Hosted API routes
- Concurrency: 1, 8, 32
- Streaming: on (default)

Required artifacts:
- Benchmark run IDs (perf suite output)
- Raw logs attached to the Confluence page ...
